prominent role in historiography, literature, and art from the 15th century on – would he not 
have known about it?’12  On its own, the evidence proffered by the Modena copy is an 
insufficient basis on which to draw definite conclusions. Yet Stichel highlights the fact that 
Giambattista’s flyleaf note refers to another copy of the HP containing marginalia, which he 
had donated to the Biblioteca Pubblica (now Comunale) of Como.13  Therefore, it was 
apparent that a study comparing the Como copy with Modena may have the potential to offer 
evidence confirming the annotator’s identity...

... In December of the same year, the Biblioteca Estense di Modena displayed this 
HP copy as part of an exhibition on Alberto Pio III of Carpi, for whose sons the young Aldus 
Manutius acted as tutor.4  The copy is largely complete.  Three leaves are lacking: the 4th, the 
229th, and the final leaf which bears the colophon and errata corrige, as recorded in an 
anonymous note in English on the back board.5 Though this most commonly excised leaf is 
remaining, the likewise oft-removed Sacrifice to Priapus leaf has stayed in place.  The 
annotator himself remarked on this fact, noting...

31 – Codex 99: The Hypnerotomachia Poliphili website, n. 4. This website
uses folio to refer both to the format of the book and to the individual
leaves of the book, which can be confusing. Hence, I have distin-
guished here between them by using folio to refer to the format and
leaf to refer to the individual sheet comprising two pages, recto and
verso.
